revision.text:
[[ファイル:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|奥田松柏軒『女用訓蒙図彙』にある長持の画]] '''長持'''（ながもち）は、主に[[近世]]の[[日本]]で用いられた[[民具]]の一つで、[[衣類]]や[[寝具]]の収納に使用された長方形の[[箱|木箱]]。
== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった。
一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ。移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、{{和暦|1657}}に[[江戸]]で発生した大火災・[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった。
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[岩井宏實]]監修|title=絵引 民具の事典|year=2008|publisher=[[河出書房新社]]|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本民具学会編|title=日本民具事典|year=1997|publisher=[[ぎょうせい]]|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:なかもち}} [[Category:民具]] [[Category:箱]]

[[ファイル:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|奥田松柏軒『女用訓蒙図彙』にある長持の画]] '''長持'''（ながもち）は、主に[[近世]]の[[日本]]で用いられた[[民具]]の一つで、[[衣類]]や[[寝具]]の収納に使用された長方形の[[箱|木箱]]。箱の下に車輪を付けて移動の便をはかったものを、車長持という。
== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった。
一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ。
移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、[[明暦]]3年（[[1657年]]）に[[江戸]]で発生した[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された。他地方では引き続き用いられ、[[宝暦]]4年（[[1754年]]）の仙台では火災のたびに車長持が引き回される状況があった<ref>『源貞氏耳袋』第7巻85（96頁）。</ref>。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[岩井宏實]]監修|title=絵引 民具の事典|year=2008|publisher=[[河出書房新社]]|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本民具学会編|title=日本民具事典|year=1997|publisher=[[ぎょうせい]]|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0}} *吉田正志・監修、「源貞氏耳袋」刊行会・編『[[源貞氏耳袋]]』第7巻、2007年。
{{DEFAULTSORT:なかもち}} [[Category:民具]] [[Category:箱]]

[[ファイル:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|奥田松柏軒『女用訓蒙図彙』にある長持の画]] '''長持'''（ながもち）は、主に[[近世]]の[[日本]]で用いられた[[民具]]の一つで、[[衣類]]や[[寝具]]の収納に使用された長方形の[[箱|木箱]]。箱の下に車輪を付けて移動の便をはかったものを、車長持という。
== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった。
一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ。
移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、[[明暦]]3年（[[1657年]]）に[[江戸]]で発生した[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された。他地方では引き続き用いられ、[[宝暦]]4年（[[1754年]]）の仙台では火災のたびに車長持が引き回される状況があった<ref>『源貞氏耳袋』第7巻85（96頁）。</ref>。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった。

[[ファイル:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|奥田松柏軒『女用訓蒙図彙』にある長持の画]] '''長持'''（ながもち）は、主に[[近世]]の[[日本]]で用いられた[[民具]]の一つで、[[衣類]]や[[寝具]]の収納に使用された長方形の[[箱|木箱]]た<ref name="岩井監修2008">[[#岩井監修2008|岩井監修2008年]]、197頁。</ref>。箱の下に車輪を付けて移動の便をはかったものを、車長持という。
== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。
一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある<ref name="朝岡他編1997">[[#朝岡他編1997|朝岡他編1997年]]、408頁。</ref>。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、[[明暦]]3年（[[1657年]]）に[[江戸]]で発生した[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。他地方では引き続き用いられ、[[宝暦]]4年（[[1754年]]）の仙台では火災のたびに車長持が引き回される状況があった<ref>[[#吉田監修2007|吉田監修2007年]]、96頁。</ref>。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。

一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある<ref name="朝岡他編1997">[[#朝岡他編1997|朝岡他編1997年]]、408頁。</ref>。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。

revision.comment: /* 概要 */ 最近の長持について
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|奥田松柏軒『女用訓蒙図彙』にある長持の画]] '''長持'''（ながもち）は、主に[[近世]]の[[日本]]で用いられた[[民具]]の一つで、[[衣類]]や[[寝具]]の収納に使用された長方形の[[箱|木箱]]<ref name="岩井監修2008">[[#岩井監修2008|岩井監修2008年]]、197頁。</ref>。箱の下に車輪を付けて移動の便をはかったものを、車長持という。
== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。
一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある<ref name="朝岡他編1997">[[#朝岡他編1997|朝岡他編1997年]]、408頁。</ref>。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、[[明暦]]3年（[[1657年]]）に[[江戸]]で発生した[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。他地方では引き続き用いられ、[[宝暦]]4年（[[1754年]]）の仙台では火災のたびに車長持が引き回される状況があった<ref>[[#吉田監修2007|吉田監修2007年]]、96頁。</ref>。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
最近は、蓋があると上に物を乗せることができず不便なため、蓋のあるものから蓋がなく側面に引き戸がついているものに変わってきている。しかし、これを「長持」と言っていいのかどうかは甚だ疑問ではあるが、婚礼の際に長持に代わる場合がある。

revision.comment: [[WP:AWW]] - 「最近は」とはいつのことか? また「甚だ疑問ではあるが」は編集者の個人的な意見に見えなくもない([[WP:NOR]])

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|奥田松柏軒『女用訓蒙図彙』にある長持の画]] '''長持'''（ながもち）は、主に[[近世]]の[[日本]]で用いられた[[民具]]の一つで、[[衣類]]や[[寝具]]の収納に使用された長方形の[[箱|木箱]]<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|岩井監修|2008|p=197}}</ref>。箱の下に車輪を付けて移動の便をはかったものを、車長持という。
== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。
一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|朝岡他編|1997|p=408}}</ref>。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、[[明暦]]3年（[[1657年]]）に[[江戸]]で発生した[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。他地方では引き続き用いられ、[[宝暦]]4年（[[1754年]]）の仙台では火災のたびに車長持が引き回される状況があった<ref>{{Harvnb|吉田監修|2007|p=96}}</ref>。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=[[朝岡康二]]他編|title=日本民具事典|year=1997|publisher=[[ぎょうせい]]|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0|ref={{SfnRef|朝岡他編|1997}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|others=[[岩井宏實]]監修|title=絵引 民具の事典|year=2008|publisher=[[河出書房新社]]|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|岩井監修|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|others=吉田正志監修|editor=源貞氏耳袋刊行会編|title=源貞氏耳袋|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|volume=第7巻|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|吉田監修|2007}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[油箪]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:なかもち}} [[Category:民具]] [[Category:箱]]

== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。 [[ファイル:Nagamochi kuruma.jpg|サムネイル|車長持]] 一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。[[鍵 (道具)|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|朝岡他編|1997|p=408}}</ref>。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、[[明暦]]3年（[[1657年]]）に[[江戸]]で発生した[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。他地方では引き続き用いられ、[[宝暦]]4年（[[1754年]]）の仙台では火災のたびに車長持が引き回される状況があった<ref>{{Harvnb|吉田監修|2007|p=96}}</ref>。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}} [[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]] A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類}} and {{ill|bedding|ja|寝具}}.<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).
== Overview == Before the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, [[:en:Chest_(furniture)|chests]] were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> [[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']] The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equipped with a hinged cover and often featured a {{ill|lock|ja|錠前}}. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|ja|明暦の大火}} in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in {{ill|1657|ja|1657年}}, the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}} in {{ill|1754|ja|1754年}} (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>
Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the {{ill|tansu|ja|箪笥}}.<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

(eds.)|1997}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Iwai Hiroshi|ja|岩井宏實}}|title=絵引 民具の事典 (Illustrated Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=2008|publisher={{ill|Kawade Shobo Shinsha|ja|河出書房新社}}|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|Iwai (ed.)|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Yoshida Masashi|ja|吉田正志}}|title=源貞氏耳袋 第7巻 (Minamoto Sadashi's Journal, Vol. 7)|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|Yoshida (ed.)|2007}}}}

{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}} [[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]] A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類}} and {{ill|bedding|ja|寝具}}.<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).
== Overview == Before the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, [[:en:Chest_(furniture)|chests]] were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> [[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']] The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equipped with a hinged cover and often featured a {{ill|lock|ja|錠前}}. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|ja|明暦の大火}} in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in {{ill|1657|ja|1657年}}, the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}} in {{ill|1754|ja|1754年}} (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>
Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the {{ill|tansu|ja|箪笥}}.<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|民具|平塚市の地名|長持 (平塚市)}} [[ファイル:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|奥田松柏軒『女用訓蒙図彙』にある長持の画]] '''長持'''（ながもち）は、主に[[近世]]の[[日本]]で用いられた[[民具]]の一つで、[[衣類]]や[[寝具]]の収納に使用された長方形の[[箱|木箱]]<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|岩井監修|2008|p=197}}</ref>。箱の下に車輪を付けて移動の便をはかったものを、車長持という。
== 概要 == [[室町時代]]以前には収納具として[[櫃]]（ひつ）が用いられていたが、時代が進むにつれて調度品や衣類が増え、さらに[[江戸時代]]には[[木綿]]が普及したことで[[掻巻]]や[[布団]]など寝具が大型化し、より大型の収納具が必要とされたことで[[武家]]で長持が使用され始め、やがて庶民の間にも普及するようになった<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。 [[ファイル:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|サムネイル|車長持]] [[ファイル:Nagamochi kuruma.jpg|right|thumb|車長持]] 一般的な大きさは、長さ8尺5寸（約174センチメートル）前後、幅と高さは2尺5寸（約75センチメートル）<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。[[錠前|錠]]を備えたかぶせ蓋がある。上等の品は[[漆]]塗り、[[家紋]]入りのものもある<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|朝岡他編|1997|p=408}}</ref>。長端部には棹（さお）を通すための金具があり、運搬時はここに太い棹（長持棹）を通して2人で担ぎ、持ち運ぶ<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
移動しやすいように底部に車輪を組み込んだ車長持が普及したこともあったが、[[明暦]]3年（[[1657年]]）に[[江戸]]で発生した[[明暦の大火]]で、家々から外へ運び出した車長持が路上にあふれ、人々の避難を妨げるという事態が生じたため、江戸・[[京都]]・[[大阪]]で使用が禁止された<ref name="岩井監修2008" />。一方、地方では引き続き用いられ、[[宝暦]]4年（[[1754年]]）の仙台では火災のたびに車長持が引き回される状況があった<ref>{{Harvnb|吉田監修|2007|p=96}}</ref>。
長持は代表的な嫁入り道具の一つでもあり、嫁入りに際して長持を運ぶ際の祝い歌は「長持歌」として伝承されたが、[[明治]]時代・[[大正]]時代以降、長持の役割は[[箪笥]]に譲られることとなった<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=[[朝岡康二]]他編|title=日本民具事典|year=1997|publisher=[[ぎょうせい]]|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0|ref={{SfnRef|朝岡他編|1997}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|others=[[岩井宏實]]監修|title=絵引 民具の事典|year=2008|publisher=[[河出書房新社]]|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|岩井監修|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|others=吉田正志監修|editor=源貞氏耳袋刊行会編|title=源貞氏耳袋|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|volume=第7巻|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|吉田監修|2007}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[油箪]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:なかもち}} [[Category:民具]] [[Category:箱]]

== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=[[朝岡康二]]他編|title=日本民具事典|year=1997|publisher=[[ぎょうせい]]|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0|ref={{SfnRef|朝岡他編|1997}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|others=[[岩井宏實]]監修|title=絵引 民具の事典|year=2008|publisher=[[河出書房新社]]|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|岩井監修|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|others=吉田正志監修|editor=源貞氏耳袋刊行会編|title=源貞氏耳袋|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|volume=第7巻|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|吉田監修|2007}}}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11653013]]
Wikidata name: nagamochi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}}
[[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]]
A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460}} and [[:en:Bedding|bedding]].<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).

== Overview ==
Before the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Chest_(furniture)|chests]] were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|qq=|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" />
[[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']]
The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a [[:en:Lock and key|lock]]. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|Great Fire of Meireki]] in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in [[:en:1657|1657]], the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]] in [[:en:1754|1754]] (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>

Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the [[:en:Commode|tansu]].<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

== References ==
{{reflist}}

== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Asaoka Koji|ja|朝岡康二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaoka Koji|qq=|1=Kōji Asaoka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20038436}} et al.|title=日本民具事典 (Japanese Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=1997|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|qq=|1=Gyosei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265295}}|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0|ref={{SfnRef|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997}}}}
* {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Iwai Hiroshi|ja|岩井宏實|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwai Hiroshi|qq=|1=Hiromi Iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474045}}|title=絵引 民具の事典 (Illustrated Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=2008|publisher=[[:en:Kawade Shobō Shinsha|Kawade Shobo Shinsha]]|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|Iwai (ed.)|2008}}}}
* {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Yoshida Masashi|ja|吉田正志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Masashi|qq=|1=Masashi Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413928}}|title=源貞氏耳袋 第7巻 (Minamoto Sadashi's Journal, Vol. 7)|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|Yoshida (ed.)|2007}}}}

== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Abura-dansu|ja|油箪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abura-dansu|qq=|1=yutan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554380}}

[[Category:Completion tier 5]]

}}

(eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

However, after the devastating [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|Great Fire of Meireki]] in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in [[:en:1657|1657]], the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]] in [[:en:1754|1754]] (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>

Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the [[:en:Commode|tansu]].<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653013]]
Wikidata name: nagamochi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}}
[[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]]
A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460}} and [[:en:Bedding|bedding]].<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).

== Overview ==
Before the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Chest_(furniture)|chests]] were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" />
[[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']]
The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a [[:en:Lock and key|lock]]. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|Great Fire of Meireki]] in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in [[:en:1657|1657]], the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]] in [[:en:1754|1754]] (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>

Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the [[:en:Commode|tansu]].<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653013]]
Wikidata name: nagamochi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}}
[[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]]
A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460}} and [[:en:Bedding|bedding]].<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).

== Overview ==
Before the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Chest_(furniture)|chests]] were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" />
[[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']]
The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a [[:en:Lock and key|lock]]. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|Great Fire of Meireki]] in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in [[:en:1657|1657]], the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]] in [[:en:1754|1754]] (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>

Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the [[:en:Commode|tansu]].<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653013]]
Wikidata name: nagamochi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}}
[[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]]
A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460}} and [[:en:Bedding|bedding]].<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).

== Overview ==
Before the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Chest_(furniture)|chests]] were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" />
[[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']]
The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a [[:en:Lock and key|lock]]. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|Great Fire of Meireki]] in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in [[:en:1657|1657]], the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]] in [[:en:1754|1754]] (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>

Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the [[:en:Commode|tansu]].<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

However, by the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the [[:en:Commode|tansu]].<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653013]]
Wikidata name: nagamochi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}}
[[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]]
A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460}} and [[:en:Bedding|bedding]].<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).

== Overview ==
Before the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Chest_(furniture)|chests]] were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" />
[[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']]
The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a [[:en:Lock and key|lock]]. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|Great Fire of Meireki]] in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in [[:en:1657|1657]], the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]] in [[:en:1754|1754]] (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>

Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the [[:en:Commode|tansu]].<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />

The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|en|Great Fire of Meireki|WD=Q1336847|lt=Great Fire of Meireki}} in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657}}, the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai}} in {{ill|1754|en|1754|WD=Q7623|lt=1754}} (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>
Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}} and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the {{ill|Commode|en|Commode|WD=Q622360|lt=tansu}}.<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
== References == {{reflist}}

(eds.)|1997}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Iwai Hiroshi|ja|岩井宏實|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwai Hiroshi|1=Hiromi Iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474045}}|title=絵引 民具の事典 (Illustrated Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=2008|publisher={{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|en|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|WD=Q11141263|lt=Kawade Shobo Shinsha}}|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|Iwai (ed.)|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Yoshida Masashi|ja|吉田正志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Masashi|1=Masashi Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413928}}|title=源貞氏耳袋 第7巻 (Minamoto Sadashi's Journal, Vol. 7)|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|Yoshida (ed.)|2007}}}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11653013]] Wikidata name: nagamochi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}} [[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]] A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460}} and {{ill|Bedding|en|Bedding|WD=Q1762457|lt=bedding}}.<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).
== Overview == Before the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Chest_(furniture)|en|Chest_(furniture)|WD=Q366134|lt=chests}} were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> [[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']] The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a {{ill|Lock and key|en|Lock and key|WD=Q228039|lt=lock}}. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|en|Great Fire of Meireki|WD=Q1336847|lt=Great Fire of Meireki}} in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657}}, the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai}} in {{ill|1754|en|1754|WD=Q7623|lt=1754}} (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>
Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}} and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the {{ill|Commode|en|Commode|WD=Q622360|lt=tansu}}.<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Asaoka Koji|ja|朝岡康二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaoka Koji|1=Kōji Asaoka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20038436}} et al.|title=日本民具事典 (Japanese Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=1997|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|1=Gyosei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265295}}|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0|ref={{SfnRef|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Iwai Hiroshi|ja|岩井宏實|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwai Hiroshi|1=Hiromi Iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474045}}|title=絵引 民具の事典 (Illustrated Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=2008|publisher={{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|en|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|WD=Q11141263|lt=Kawade Shobo Shinsha}}|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|Iwai (ed.)|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Yoshida Masashi|ja|吉田正志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Masashi|1=Masashi Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413928}}|title=源貞氏耳袋 第7巻 (Minamoto Sadashi's Journal, Vol. 7)|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|Yoshida (ed.)|2007}}}}

{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}} [[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]] A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460}} and {{ill|Bedding|en|Bedding|WD=Q1762457|lt=bedding}}.<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).
== Overview == Before the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Chest_(furniture)|en|Chest_(furniture)|WD=Q366134|lt=chests}} were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> [[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']] The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a {{ill|Lock and key|en|Lock and key|WD=Q228039|lt=lock}}. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|en|Great Fire of Meireki|WD=Q1336847|lt=Great Fire of Meireki}} in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657}}, the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai}} in {{ill|1754|en|1754|WD=Q7623|lt=1754}} (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>
Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}} and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the {{ill|Commode|en|Commode|WD=Q622360|lt=tansu}}.<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Asaoka Koji|ja|朝岡康二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaoka Koji|1=Kōji Asaoka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20038436}} et al.|title=日本民具事典 (Japanese Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=1997|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|1=Gyosei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265295}}|isbn=978-4-324-03912-0|ref={{SfnRef|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Iwai Hiroshi|ja|岩井宏實|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwai Hiroshi|1=Hiromi Iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474045}}|title=絵引 民具の事典 (Illustrated Folk Tools Encyclopedia)|year=2008|publisher={{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|en|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|WD=Q11141263|lt=Kawade Shobo Shinsha}}|isbn=978-4-309-22487-9|ref={{SfnRef|Iwai (ed.)|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|editor={{ill|Yoshida Masashi|ja|吉田正志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Masashi|1=Masashi Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413928}}|title=源貞氏耳袋 第7巻 (Minamoto Sadashi's Journal, Vol. 7)|year=2007|publisher=源貞氏耳袋刊行会|ncid=BA83539010|ref={{SfnRef|Yoshida (ed.)|2007}}}}

<!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--><!--Nagamochi--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11653013}} [[Category:Boxes]] [[Category:Folk crafts]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長持]]
{{Otheruses|folk implements|place name in Hiratsuka City|Nagamochi (Hiratsuka City)}} [[File:Shohakuken_Nagamochi.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Illustration of a nagamochi from Okuda Shōhakuken's *Nyōyō Kunmō Zui*]] A '''nagamochi''' (長持) is a type of rectangular wooden chest used primarily during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} in Japan for storing items such as {{ill|clothing|ja|衣類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clothing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 被服|1=Clothing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11460}} and {{ill|Bedding|en|Bedding|qid=Q1762457|lt=bedding|1=Bedding}}.<ref name="岩井監修2008">{{Harvnb|Iwai (ed.)|2008|p=197}}</ref> When wheels were attached to its base to improve mobility, it was referred to as a ''kurumanagamochi'' (車長持).
== Overview == Before the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Chest_(furniture)|en|Chest_(furniture)|qid=Q366134|lt=chests|1=Chest (furniture)}} were commonly used for storage. However, with the increasing abundance of household items and clothing, coupled with the popularization of cotton during the Edo period, bedding items such as {{ill|kakebuton|ja|掻巻|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kakebuton|1=kaimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498380}} and futons became larger. This necessitated the use of larger storage furniture, leading to the adoption of the nagamochi by {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Buke|qid=Q2927809}} households, and later its spread among commoners.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> [[File:Kuruma-Nagamochi at Saga Prefectural Museum.jpg|thumb|A ''kurumanagamochi'']] The typical dimensions of a nagamochi were approximately 8.5 shaku (about 174 centimeters) in length and 2.5 shaku (about 75 centimeters) in both width and height.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> The chest was equi11ed with a hinged cover and often featured a {{ill|Lock and key|en|Lock and key|qid=Q228039|lt=lock|1=Lock and key}}. High-quality nagamochi were lacquered and sometimes decorated with family crests.<ref name="朝岡他編1997">{{Harvnb|Asaoka et al. (eds.)|1997|p=408}}</ref> Metal fittings on the shorter sides allowed the insertion of poles (''nagamochizao''), enabling two people to carry the chest.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /><ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
The ''kurumanagamochi,'' fitted with wheels for easier transport, gained popularity. However, after the devastating {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|en|Great Fire of Meireki|qid=Q1336847|lt=Great Fire of Meireki|1=Great Fire of Meireki}} in Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in {{ill|1657|en|1657|qid=Q6987|lt=1657|1=1657}}, the use of these wheeled chests was banned in Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka due to their tendency to congest roads during evacuations.<ref name="岩井監修2008" /> Nonetheless, their use persisted in rural areas. For instance, during fires in {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|qid=Q46747|lt=Sendai|1=Sendai}} in {{ill|1754|en|1754|qid=Q7623|lt=1754|1=1754}} (Hōreki 4), ''kurumanagamochi'' were still being moved through the streets.<ref>{{Harvnb|Yoshida (ed.)|2007|p=96}}</ref>
Nagamochi were also traditional items in bridal dowries. Songs sung during the transport of nagamochi as part of wedding celebrations, known as ''nagamochi songs'' (長持歌), have been passed down through generations. However, by the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, the nagamochi was largely replaced by the {{ill|Commode|en|Commode|qid=Q622360|lt=tansu|1=Commode}}.<ref name="朝岡他編1997" />
